Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-3). It is a dataset-agnostic BLUEPRINT engine for rigorous empirical ML/NLP. Hard rules: the engine imports NO dataset/adapter specifics (ADR-0002); all engine computation/storage/observability + the bulk reasoning tier run on-box (ADR-0003) while the optional high-stakes tier is off-box by design; optional heavy backends are lazy-imported behind extras (ADR-0006).

This is the THIRD hardening pass. Passes 1 and 2 already fixed findings #101-#156 — read docs/hardening/PASS-1.md and docs/hardening/PASS-2.md and DO NOT re-report anything already fixed there. Use /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/ruff if you want to run a PoC (read-only; do not commit). Be adversarial, precise, and concrete: every finding needs a real file:line, a crisp statement of the production-readiness defect, a PoC or mutation demonstrating it where possible, and a specific suggested fix. Prefer a few HIGH-confidence real defects over many speculative nits. If the area is genuinely clean, say so and return few/no findings.

YOUR DIMENSION: Performance & scale: O(n^2)/quadratic or accidentally-materialized structures at corpus scale, unbounded memory, redundant recomputation, pathological inputs (huge corpora, many claims, deep nesting) that degrade or OOM.

docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:- **Problem:** ARCHITECTURE §1 "Baseline toolkit" row claims the engine provides "Generic, dataset-agnostic builders: embeddings, similarity/distance, graph/topology, clustering, descriptive statistics. The raw material for any baseline." The public package docstring (src/assay_engine/__init__.py:14-15) repeats it: "baseline — dataset-agnostic baseline builders (embeddings, similarity, graph, clustering, descriptive stats)." But the engine ships NO embedding, clustering, graph, or topology builders. baseline/primitives.py provides only cosine/euclidean similarity-distance and descriptive_stats; baseline/determinism.py provides the determinism harness. The modules' OWN docstrings explicitly contradict the ARCHITECTURE/__init__ claim: primitives.py:8-12 "Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring their own builders... the engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002)."; toolkit.py:4-7 "the choice-bearing builder that assembles them — which embedding model, which clustering, which graph — encodes dataset decisions and is supplied by a study's adapter." grep confirms no kmeans/cluster/networkx/embed/graph builder anywhere in src/. So the engine-component description over-states what is shipped, and the package's public docstring is internally self-contradictory with its own submodule docstrings.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md-- **Suggested fix:** Correct ARCHITECTURE §1 and __init__.py:14-15 to describe what the engine actually ships, e.g. "Baseline toolkit: dataset-agnostic primitives (similarity/distance, descriptive statistics) and a determinism/reproducibility harness — the raw material; the choice-bearing builders (embeddings, clustering, graph/topology) are supplied per-study by the adapter's BaselineBuilder (ADR-0002)."

docs/hardening/PASS-1.md-- **Location:** `src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py` 112 (also 115-131)
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:- **Problem:** _cosine_matrix_numpy computes the n x n similarity matrix vectorized (good), then at line 112 returns `[[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]` — a full nested Python list of n^2 native float objects. A numpy float64 cell is 8 bytes; a boxed Python float is ~24 bytes plus list-pointer overhead (~8 B) and the per-row list headers, i.e. roughly 32-56 B/cell, ~4-7x the dense array. The conversion is also a pure-Python O(n^2) loop that dominates runtime at scale. PoC against the venv (numpy 2.4.6): n=3000, d=384 (MiniLM-size corpus) took 7.76 s and 390 MB of tracked Python allocation; the returned nested list alone is ~576 MB by the cell-overhead estimate. Breaking point: a corpus of ~5-10k embeddings (25M-100M cells) produces a multi-GB nested list and minutes of boxing time on a Mac mini, OOM-risking the box well before the underlying matmul would. The function signature forces the dense materialization regardless of caller need (most callers want top-k neighbors, a threshold graph, or block-wise stats — none need the full boxed matrix).
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:- **Suggested fix:** Return the numpy ndarray (or a thin read-only view) from the numpy path instead of boxing to nested lists, letting callers index without the 4-7x blow-up; or expose a streaming/blocked API (e.g. top-k per row, or a generator over row blocks) so the full n x n matrix is never materialized. If a list-of-lists must stay for the pure path's contract, gate the dense materialization behind an explicit `as_dense=True` and document that it is O(n^2) memory.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:- **Verifier 1:** The mechanism is real: src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:112 (`return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]`) boxes the dense numpy similarity matrix into a nested Python list, and the public API (cosine_similarity_matrix, line 115) is contractually list[list[float]] with no streaming/top-k/blocked alternative, so a full O(n^2) dense matrix is always materialized regardless of caller need. My PoC confirms the boxing loop (0.46s) dwarfs the actual vectorized numeric work (0.019s) at n=3000, and the nested list does cost ~4x an equivalent ndarray.\n\nBut the finding is materially over-stated, and its severity rests on a flawed benchmark:\n\n1) The "~70x peak memory" headline and "~576 MB nested list" are wrong. Measured deep size of the returned nested list at n=3000,d=384 is 294 MB vs 72 MB for the equivalent ndarray = 4.1x, not 70x. (4.1x is the textbook boxed-float-vs-float64 factor.) The "70x" appears to conflate per-cell byte ratios with something else; it is not reproducible.\n\n2) The "7.76 s" runtime (and "minutes of boxing on a Mac mini") is a tracemalloc artifact. Under tracemalloc the function takes ~7.9s; with tracemalloc off, clean wall-clock is 0.33s at n=3000 an
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:- **Verifier 2:** The code claim is accurate: src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:112 returns `[[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]`, boxing the O(n^2) numpy result into a nested Python list. The memory blow-up is real and I reproduced it: the nested list is ~4x the dense ndarray (n=6k -> ~1.15 GB list vs ~288 MB ndarray; n=10k -> ~3.2 GB list vs ~800 MB ndarray), driving peak RSS to ~2 GB at n=6k and ~5.6 GB at n=10k, a genuine OOM risk on an 8-16 GB Mac mini. So there is a legitimate efficiency/scale concern and the suggested fix (return the ndarray or expose a blocked/top-k API) is sound.

docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:- **Problem:** BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previous finding, a 5k-embedding baseline that builds an in-memory similarity matrix is materialized at least three times (numpy array -> nested list -> nested tuple). Breaking point: ~3-5k units with any dense O(n^2) content; for raw embedding matrices (n x d) it scales with n*d and a 100k-doc x 768-dim embedding set (77M cells) is hundreds of MB-to-GB of duplicated tuples plus tens of seconds of pure-Python work at artifact construction.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md-- **Suggested fix:** Do not deep-freeze large numeric payloads cell-by-cell. The freeze() already has array-like handling (lines 85-94) that canonicalizes objects exposing tobytes()/shape to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) wrappers — steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array for bulk numeric content so this fast path is taken, and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays so contents are not recursively tuple-ized. At minimum, document that `contents` must hold buffer-backed arrays (not nested Python lists) for any payload above a few thousand cells.

For an n*n nested-list payload this is O(n^2) pure-Python work plus an O(n^2) tuple allocation.

1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def duck_mapping_items(value: Any) -> list[tuple[Any, Any]] | None:
62	    """Return ``(key, value)`` items if ``value`` is a dict-like NOT registered as a Mapping.
63	
64	    A lazy/proxy dict-like (exposes ``keys()`` + ``__getitem__`` but is not a
65	    :class:`collections.abc.Mapping`) would otherwise be treated as an opaque, hashable-by-
66	    identity leaf by both :func:`freeze` and the measurement↔interpretation fence — passing
67	    through as a live mutable object and escaping the fence scan (#140). Returns ``None`` for
68	    anything that is not such a duck-typed mapping (incl. real Mappings, str/bytes, sequences).
69	    """
70	    if isinstance(value, (str, bytes, bytearray, Mapping)):
71	        return None
72	    keys = getattr(value, "keys", None)
73	    getitem = getattr(value, "__getitem__", None)
74	    if callable(keys) and callable(getitem):
75	        try:
76	            return [(k, value[k]) for k in keys()]
77	        except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — a misbehaving dict-like is not a usable mapping
78	            return None
79	    return None
80	
81	
82	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
83	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
84	
85	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
86	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
87	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
88	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
89	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
90	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
91	    """
92	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
93	        return value
94	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
95	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
96	    duck = duck_mapping_items(value)
97	    if duck is not None:  # lazy/proxy dict-like — freeze it like a mapping, not a leaf (#140)
98	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in duck})
99	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
100	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
101	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
102	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
103	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
104	        return bytes(value)
105	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
106	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
107	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
108	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
109	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
110	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
111	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
112	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
113	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
114	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
115	        )
116	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
117	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
118	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
119	    try:
120	        hash(value)
121	    except TypeError as exc:
122	        raise TypeError(
123	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
124	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
125	        ) from exc
126	    return value
127	
128	
129	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
130	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
131	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
132	
133	
134	def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
135	    """Recursively convert a frozen structure back into plain JSON-serializable containers.
136	
137	    The inverse of :func:`freeze` for serialization: :class:`FrozenDict` → ``dict``,
138	    ``frozenset`` → sorted ``list`` (stable order for reproducible output), ``tuple`` →
139	    ``list``. Leaves are returned unchanged. ``json.dumps`` does not know :class:`FrozenDict`
140	    (it is a Mapping, not a ``dict`` subclass), so an operator serializing a deep-frozen public
141	    payload — e.g. ``GateReview.payload`` — must thaw it first (#141).
142	    """
143	    if isinstance(value, (FrozenDict, Mapping)):
144	        return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
145	    if isinstance(value, frozenset):
146	        # sort when elements are mutually orderable; otherwise keep insertion-free stable repr
147	        try:
148	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value)]
149	        except TypeError:
150	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
151	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
152	        return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
153	    return value
154

1	"""Type-faithful canonical hashing — the engine's single content-digest primitive.
2	
3	Two unrelated places need a *stable, type-faithful* content hash: the baseline determinism
4	harness (so the same inputs reproduce the same baseline) and pre-registration (so a lock
5	binds the exact hypothesis content). Both must distinguish values that merely share a
6	``str()`` — ``int 1`` from ``str "1"``, ``date(2020,1,1)`` from ``"2020-01-01"`` — or the
7	hash is a liability rather than a guarantee.
8	
9	This module is the one implementation, hoisted here so it sits *below* both
10	``methodology`` and ``baseline`` in the import graph (neither may depend on the other). It is
11	dependency-free and pure. Anything it cannot canonicalize reproducibly is refused **loudly**:
12	a default (address-based) ``repr`` would make the digest differ every process and silently
13	break the guarantee it exists to provide.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import datetime as _dt
19	import decimal
20	import hashlib
21	import json
22	import uuid
23	from typing import Any, Mapping
24	
25	
26	def keytag(key: Any) -> str:
27	    """A type-faithful, sortable string for a mapping key, so distinct keys that happen to
28	    share a ``str()`` (e.g. int ``1`` vs str ``"1"``) do NOT collide."""
29	    return f"{type(key).__name__}::{key!r}"
30	
31	
32	def canonical_plain(value: Any) -> Any:
33	    """Canonicalize ``value`` into a fully JSON-native, type-faithful structure for hashing.
34	
35	    Mappings become a sorted list of ``[typed-key, value]`` pairs; bytes, sets, and any
36	    non-JSON-native leaf are tagged with their type rather than stringified. Nothing is coerced
37	    with ``str()``, so the content hash distinguishes values that share a ``str()``.
38	    """
39	    if value is None or isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float)):
40	        return value  # JSON-native scalars; json encodes 1, "1", true, 1.0 distinctly
41	    if isinstance(value, bytes):
42	        return {"__bytes__": value.hex()}
43	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
44	        pairs = sorted(
45	            ([keytag(k), canonical_plain(v)] for k, v in value.items()), key=lambda p: p[0]
46	        )
47	        return {"__map__": pairs}
48	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
49	        return [canonical_plain(v) for v in value]
50	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
51	        return {"__set__": sorted((canonical_plain(v) for v in value), key=repr)}
52	    # A small allowlist of leaf types with a value-faithful canonical form.
53	    if isinstance(value, (_dt.date, _dt.datetime, _dt.time)):
54	        return {"__temporal__": value.isoformat()}
55	    if isinstance(value, decimal.Decimal):
56	        return {"__decimal__": str(value)}
57	    if isinstance(value, uuid.UUID):
58	        return {"__uuid__": str(value)}
59	    # Anything else is refused LOUDLY rather than repr-tagged: a default (address-based) repr
60	    # would make the digest differ every process, silently breaking the guarantee.
61	    raise TypeError(
62	        f"cannot canonicalize a leaf of type "
63	        f"{type(value).__module__}.{type(value).__qualname__!r} reproducibly — convert it to a "
64	        "JSON-native value, bytes, date/datetime/Decimal/UUID before hashing"
65	    )
66	
67	
68	def canonical_json(value: Any) -> str:
69	    """The canonical JSON text of ``value`` — stable across processes and key orderings."""
70	    return json.dumps(canonical_plain(value), sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False)
71	
72	
73	def hash_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
74	    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
75	
76	
77	def hash_text(text: str) -> str:
78	    return hash_bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))
79	
80	
81	def hash_value(value: Any) -> str:
82	    """Stable, type-faithful content hash of an arbitrary value.
83	
84	    ``canonical_plain`` canonicalizes everything to JSON-native form first, so ``json.dumps``
85	    needs no ``default=`` coercion — anything it still cannot encode is a real bug to surface,
86	    not to silently stringify.
87	    """
88	    return hash_text(canonical_json(value))
89

It has NO array/buffer fast-path (unlike freeze), so a numpy array in a hashed value... actually it would hit the TypeError. Let me look at discovery, adjudication, and the determinism build path which are corpus-scale.

1	"""Discovery runner — Firewall B (discover/confirm separation) enforced by construction.
2	
3	The mirror of :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.adjudication`. In discovery mode the data surfaces
4	candidate hypotheses; Firewall B (METHODOLOGY.md §2, ADR-0005) requires that the data used to
5	*discover* a hypothesis is not the data used to *confirm* it — otherwise discovering a pattern
6	and "testing" it on the same data proves nothing.
7	
8	The engine had the split primitive (:class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`) and a confirm step that
9	refuses discovery ids, but no composition — so the separation held only if a study wired it by
10	hand (same risk Firewall A had before ADR-0008). :func:`discover_and_confirm` makes it
11	structural: it hands the ``discover`` step a corpus containing *only* the discovery partition
12	and the ``confirm`` step a corpus containing *only* the held-out partition, so neither step can
13	accidentally use the other's data.
14	
15	Honest scope (lesson from the adjudication review, ADR-0008): the engine partitions the
16	**structural** data — units, and relations whose *both* endpoints are in the partition. It
17	does NOT carry the corpus's free-form ``metadata`` into a subset (an arbitrary mapping cannot
18	be partitioned safely — a per-unit index in metadata would otherwise leak the other partition
19	wholesale), and it cannot police free-text inside ``Unit``/``Relation.attributes`` that an
20	adapter might use to reference units outside the partition. So: the structural separation is
21	enforced; keeping per-unit data out of metadata/attribute *payloads* is the adapter's
22	obligation (the Firewall-B analogue of the corpus-content caveat in ADR-0005). And, as with
23	adjudication, this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping,
24	not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could).
25	"""
26	
27	from __future__ import annotations
28	
29	import datetime as _dt
30	from typing import Callable, Iterable, Protocol
31	
32	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
33	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
34	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
35	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
36	    TimestampAuthority,
37	    require_preregistered,
38	)
39	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
40	
41	
42	class HeldOutConfirmer(Protocol):
43	    """Study-supplied: confirm one locked, data-surfaced hypothesis on the held-out corpus."""
44	
45	    def __call__(self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, held_out: Corpus) -> Verdict: ...
46	
47	
48	def subset_corpus(corpus: Corpus, ids: Iterable[str]) -> Corpus:
49	    """Return the sub-corpus of ``corpus`` restricted to ``ids``.
50	
51	    Units not in ``ids`` are dropped; a relation is kept only if *both* endpoints are in
52	    ``ids`` (a relation to a unit outside the partition would leak that unit's existence/links
53	    into the partition).
54	
55	    Corpus-level ``metadata`` is deliberately **dropped**, not carried through: it is an
56	    arbitrary mapping the engine cannot partition safely. A per-unit index living in metadata
57	    (e.g. ``{"by_unit": {unit_id: ...}}``) would otherwise hand the discovery step the held-out
58	    partition wholesale — silently defeating Firewall B. Adapters that need partition-scoped
59	    side data must put it on the ``Unit``/``Relation`` records (which *are* partitioned), not in
60	    corpus metadata; free-text inside ``attributes`` that references foreign units remains the
61	    adapter's obligation (ADR-0005 / ADR-0008 honest-scope caveat).
62	    """
63	    keep = frozenset(ids)
64	    units = tuple(u for u in corpus.units if u.unit_id in keep)
65	    relations = tuple(r for r in corpus.relations if r.source_id in keep and r.target_id in keep)
66	    return Corpus(units=units, relations=relations)
67	
68	
69	def discover_and_confirm(
70	    corpus: Corpus,
71	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
72	    *,
73	    discover: Callable[[Corpus], Iterable[Hypothesis]],
74	    confirm: HeldOutConfirmer,
75	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
76	) -> list[Verdict]:
77	    """Run discovery then confirmation with the data separation enforced by construction.
78	
79	    1. ``discover`` is handed a corpus containing only the discovery partition and returns
80	       candidate hypotheses. They must be ``DISCOVERY``-origin and genuinely **pre-registered**
81	       before confirmation — ``require_preregistered`` checks (via ``authority``) that the proof
82	       binds each hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes confirmation.
83	       Discovering and confirming on the same data, or confirming a hypothesis whose content was
84	       changed after locking, is the double-dipping Firewall B exists to prevent.
85	    2. ``confirm`` is handed a corpus containing only the held-out partition and tests each
86	       hypothesis there; the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers.
87	
88	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
89	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
90	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default. A study's ``discover`` step locks each
91	    discovered hypothesis (e.g. via :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.
92	    lock_hypothesis`) before returning it.
93	
94	    Returns the verdicts. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl. ``PreRegistrationError``) on an
95	    empty discovery or held-out partition (split ids that select no corpus units — a vacuous
96	    test), a non-``DISCOVERY`` hypothesis, a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered
97	    before confirmation, or a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution.
98	    """
99	    discovery_corpus = subset_corpus(corpus, split.discovery_ids)
100	    if not discovery_corpus.units:
101	        # the discovery partition selects no actual corpus units — a hypothesis "discovered"
102	        # from zero data is not data-surfaced at all; it would confirm cleanly on the held-out
103	        # set and silently launder a hand-authored claim through Firewall B. Fail loud.
104	        raise FirewallViolation(
105	            "discovery partition selects no corpus units — nothing to discover from "
106	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
107	        )
108	    hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))  # sees only the discovery partition
109	
110	    held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)  # disjoint from discovery (split invariant)
111	    if not held_out.units:
112	        # the confirmation partition selects no actual corpus units — confirming on an empty
113	        # held-out set is a vacuous test (the split ids don't match the corpus). Fail loud.
114	        raise FirewallViolation(
115	            "confirmation partition selects no corpus units — nothing to confirm on "
116	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
117	        )
118	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
119	    for hypothesis in hypotheses:
120	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY:
121	            raise FirewallViolation(
122	                f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is {hypothesis.origin.value!r}; the "
123	                "discovery runner expects DISCOVERY-origin (data-surfaced) hypotheses"
124	            )
125	        # Pre-registration enforced by the runner: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content
126	        # (no post-lock content swap) and its attested lock time must precede this confirmation.
127	        require_preregistered(
128	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
129	        )
130	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
131	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
132	            raise FirewallViolation(
133	                f"verdict reports hypothesis_id {verdict.hypothesis_id!r} for hypothesis "
134	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
135	            )
136	        verdicts.append(verdict)
137	    return verdicts
138

1	"""Determinism & reproducibility harness for the baseline (ADR-0001, METHODOLOGY.md §1, §7).
2	
3	A baseline is only a yardstick if the *same inputs reproduce the same baseline*. This module
4	makes that contract concrete and engine-level: it content-hashes inputs, derives seeds
5	deterministically from those hashes, records component versions, and stamps all of it into a
6	:class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineArtifact`'s ``determinism`` record — so a
7	hostile reviewer can confirm a baseline was produced exactly as claimed.
8	
9	It is dependency-free and pure. The heavy, *choice-bearing* builders (which embedding model,
10	which clustering algorithm, which graph construction) encode dataset/study decisions and are
11	supplied by a study's adapter as :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`
12	implementations — not prescribed by the engine (ADR-0002). This harness records *how* such a
13	builder ran so the result is reproducible regardless of which builder it was.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import hashlib
19	import json
20	import sys
21	from dataclasses import dataclass
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION
25	
26	# The canonical hashing primitives moved to ``assay_engine._canonical`` (see note below); these
27	# imports re-export them at their historical home so existing call sites keep working.
28	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_plain as _plain  # noqa: F401
29	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_bytes as hash_bytes  # noqa: F401  (re-export)
30	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_text
31	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_value as hash_value  # re-export (baseline public API)
32	from assay_engine._canonical import keytag as _keytag  # noqa: F401
33	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
34	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
35	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
36	
37	# The canonical hashing primitives (``_plain``/``_keytag``/``hash_bytes``/``hash_text``/
38	# ``hash_value``) live in ``assay_engine._canonical`` so they sit below both ``baseline`` and
39	# ``methodology`` in the import graph (audit #B1/#B2/#B5 originally landed here; hoisted so
40	# pre-registration can content-bind a hypothesis with the *same* type-faithful digest). They are
41	# re-exported above for back-compat.
42	
43	
44	def corpus_fingerprint(corpus: Corpus) -> str:
45	    """Deterministic content hash of a corpus (order-independent across units/relations)."""
46	    units = [
47	        {"id": u.unit_id, "text": u.text, "attrs": _plain(u.attributes)}
48	        for u in sorted(corpus.units, key=lambda u: u.unit_id)
49	    ]
50	    relations = [
51	        {"s": r.source_id, "t": r.target_id, "k": r.kind, "attrs": _plain(r.attributes)}
52	        for r in sorted(corpus.relations, key=lambda r: (r.source_id, r.target_id, r.kind))
53	    ]
54	    payload = {"units": units, "relations": relations, "metadata": _plain(corpus.metadata)}
55	    return hash_text(json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False))
56	
57	
58	def stable_seed(*parts: str, bits: int = 32) -> int:
59	    """A deterministic non-negative integer seed derived from ``parts``.
60	
61	    Derived from a hash so the same inputs always yield the same seed — reproducible without a
62	    hard-coded magic number, and distinct inputs get distinct seeds.
63	    """
64	    digest = hashlib.sha256("\x1f".join(parts).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
65	    return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)
66	
67	
68	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
69	class DeterminismRecord:
70	    """The reproducibility provenance stamped onto a baseline (ADR-0001)."""
71	
72	    seed: int
73	    input_hashes: Mapping[str, str]
74	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        object.__setattr__(self, "input_hashes", freeze_mapping(self.input_hashes))
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "component_versions", freeze_mapping(self.component_versions))
79	
80	    def as_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
81	        return {
82	            "seed": self.seed,
83	            "input_hashes": dict(self.input_hashes),
84	            "component_versions": dict(self.component_versions),
85	        }
86	
87	
88	def build_baseline_artifact(
89	    corpus: Corpus,
90	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
91	    *,
92	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
93	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
94	    seed: int | None = None,
95	    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
96	    trust_corpus_hash: bool = False,
97	) -> BaselineArtifact:
98	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
99	
100	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
101	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
102	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
103	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
104	    so it is reproducible.
105	
106	    ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed :func:`corpus_fingerprint` (#119). By
107	    default it is **verified** against the corpus and a mismatch raises ``ValueError`` — a
108	    stale/typo'd hash would otherwise produce a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived
109	    seed describe a *different* corpus than the one passed, silently defeating the reproducibility
110	    guarantee this harness exists to provide (#155). Pass ``trust_corpus_hash=True`` to skip the
111	    recompute (the #119 optimization) only when the caller computed the hash from this exact
112	    corpus and accepts integrity responsibility.
113	    """
114	    if corpus_hash is None:
115	        corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
116	    elif not trust_corpus_hash:
117	        # Verify the supplied hash binds the corpus actually passed (default-safe, #155).
118	        actual = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
119	        if corpus_hash != actual:
120	            raise ValueError(
121	                f"corpus_hash {corpus_hash!r} does not match the corpus "
122	                f"(corpus_fingerprint={actual!r}) — the determinism record would bind the wrong "
123	                "data; pass trust_corpus_hash=True only if you accept integrity responsibility"
124	            )
125	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
126	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
127	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
128	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
129	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
130	
131	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
132	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
133	
134	    if seed is None:
135	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
136	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
137	
138	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
139	    return BaselineArtifact(
140	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
141	    )
142

1	"""Adjudication runner — the engine owns the firewall guarantee, not each study (ADR-0008).
2	
3	METHODOLOGY.md describes the adjudication pipeline: build a baseline *blind* to the external
4	claims (Firewall A), convert each claim into a typed, pre-stated hypothesis, test each against
5	the blind baseline, and *score the external source* against the validated baseline (§5). Until
6	now the engine shipped the parts (a custodial :class:`ClaimBlindGuard`, the confirmatory tests,
7	the verdicts) but no composition — so Firewall A held only if a study wired it correctly by
8	hand. For a blueprint inherited by many studies, that pushes the single most important
9	methodological guarantee onto every cloner, where it is easy to get silently wrong (build the
10	baseline with the claims in scope and nothing stops you).
11	
12	:func:`adjudicate` makes the guarantee structural: it builds the baseline *inside* a sealed
13	claim-guard that holds the claims source, so the baseline builder cannot reach the claims
14	during construction (any attempt raises ``FirewallViolation``); only after the baseline is
15	built does the runner release the claims and adjudicate. It then aggregates the verdicts into a
16	:class:`SourceScorecard` — the §5 "score the source" step, expressed as alignment *frequency*
17	on decisive claims, explicitly NOT a normative judgement of the source's quality (richer
18	dimensions — where it aligns, diverges, leaves gaps — are data-surfaced downstream, per §5).
19	"""
20	
21	from __future__ import annotations
22	
23	import datetime as _dt
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Callable, Iterable, Protocol
26	
27	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ClaimRecord, ExternalClaimsSource
28	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
29	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard, FirewallViolation
30	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
31	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
32	    TimestampAuthority,
33	    require_preregistered,
34	)
35	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
36	
37	if TYPE_CHECKING:
38	    # Annotations only — importing baseline at runtime would invert the layering
39	    # (baseline.toolkit depends on methodology.firewalls) and create a cycle.
40	    from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact, BaselineBuilder
41	
42	
43	class ClaimConfirmer(Protocol):
44	    """Study-supplied: confirm one claim-derived hypothesis against the (blind) baseline.
45	
46	    The study owns this because only it knows the baseline's structure and how a given claim
47	    maps onto a measurement of it; the engine supplies the confirmatory primitives
48	    (``confirm_whole_corpus`` / ``confirm_unit_level``) the study uses inside it.
49	    """
50	
51	    def __call__(
52	        self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, baseline: BaselineArtifact, claim: ClaimRecord
53	    ) -> Verdict: ...
54	
55	
56	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
57	class SourceScorecard:
58	    """How an external source aligns with the independent baseline (METHODOLOGY.md §5).
59	
60	    ``alignment_rate`` is supported / (supported + contradicted) — the fraction of *decisive*
61	    claims where the blind baseline agrees with the source's assertion. ``indeterminate`` is
62	    excluded from the denominator (the method could not decide; counting it would understate
63	    or overstate alignment). This is a frequency, not a verdict on whether the source is
64	    "good": it says how often an independent reading of the data corroborates the source.
65	    """
66	
67	    source: str
68	    n_supported: int
69	    n_contradicted: int
70	    n_indeterminate: int
71	    alignment_rate: float | None
72	    verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
73	
74	    @property
75	    def total(self) -> int:
76	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted + self.n_indeterminate
77	
78	    @property
79	    def decisive(self) -> int:
80	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted
81	
82	
83	def _score(source: str, verdicts: list[Verdict]) -> SourceScorecard:
84	    counts = {
85	        VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED: 0,
86	        VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED: 0,
87	        VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE: 0,
88	    }
89	    for v in verdicts:
90	        counts[v.label] += 1
91	    decisive = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] + counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED]
92	    rate = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] / decisive if decisive else None
93	    return SourceScorecard(
94	        source=source,
95	        n_supported=counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED],
96	        n_contradicted=counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED],
97	        n_indeterminate=counts[VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE],
98	        alignment_rate=rate,
99	        verdicts=tuple(verdicts),
100	    )
101	
102	
103	def adjudicate(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource,
106	    *,
107	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder,
108	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
109	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
110	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
111	    source_name: str = "external",
112	) -> tuple[BaselineArtifact, SourceScorecard]:
113	    """Run the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction.
114	
115	    1. The claims source is kept in this function's local scope and is NOT handed to the
116	       builder; the baseline is built inside a sealed :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` that holds
117	       nothing. This is a **signature-level** guarantee: the builder is never *handed* a
118	       claims source by the engine, so it cannot accidentally consult the claims. It is NOT
119	       frame isolation — a builder that deliberately reflects into this runner's call stack
120	       (``sys._getframe``) could reach ``claims_source``; preventing that would require
121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is
122	       against *accidental* circularity, not against a builder that willfully defeats the
123	       firewall.
124	    2. After the baseline exists, each claim is mapped (by ``hypothesis_for``) to its typed,
125	       pre-stated ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` hypothesis whose ``source_claim_id`` must match the claim
126	       and which must be genuinely **pre-registered** — :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.
127	       preregistration.require_preregistered` checks (via the supplied ``authority``) that the
128	       proof binds this hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes the
129	       **baseline build** (the ordering instant is captured before the build). A claim-derived
130	       hypothesis must therefore be locked *before* ``adjudicate`` is called — ``hypothesis_for``
131	       looks up an already-locked hypothesis; locking on demand inside it (after the baseline
132	       exists) is rejected, so the hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline.
133	    3. The verdict's ``hypothesis_id`` must match the hypothesis it answers (no misattribution),
134	       and the verdicts are aggregated into a :class:`SourceScorecard`.
135	
136	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
137	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
138	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default, so a study must supply a real one.
139	
140	    Returns ``(baseline, scorecard)``. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl.
141	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.PreRegistrationError`) on any
142	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity mismatch, a non-``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` (data-surfaced)
143	    hypothesis, or a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered before confirmation.
144	    """
145	    # The claims source stays in THIS function's local scope and is never given to the builder.
146	    # The builder receives a guard holding nothing (a blind-mode signal only), so there is no
147	    # object reachable from the builder that contains the claims — not even via the guard's
148	    # internals. This is stronger than a custodial guard, whose private `_claims_source` a
149	    # determined builder could read past the sealed `release()` check (adversarial review).
150	    # Captured BEFORE the baseline is built: an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis derives from the
151	    # external claim, not from the baseline, so it must be pre-registered before the baseline (the
152	    # answer) exists. Using this as `not_after` makes "lock before the result is in hand" real —
153	    # a hypothesis locked on demand inside `hypothesis_for` (i.e. after the build) is rejected,
154	    # forcing studies to pre-lock their claim-derived hypotheses (#83).
155	    not_after = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
156	
157	    guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource] = ClaimBlindGuard()
158	    with guard.sealed():
159	        baseline = baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
160	
161	    scorecard = adjudicate_with_baseline(
162	        baseline,
163	        claims_source.claims(),
164	        hypothesis_for=hypothesis_for,
165	        confirm=confirm,
166	        authority=authority,
167	        not_after=not_after,
168	        source_name=source_name,
169	    )
170	    return baseline, scorecard
171	
172	
173	def adjudicate_with_baseline(
174	    baseline: BaselineArtifact,
175	    claims: "Iterable[ClaimRecord]",
176	    *,
177	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
178	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
179	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
180	    not_after: _dt.datetime,
181	    source_name: str = "external",
182	    on_step: "Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None" = None,
183	) -> SourceScorecard:
184	    """Adjudicate a **materialized claim set** against an already-built blind baseline (core).
185	
186	    Factored out of :func:`adjudicate` so a composed pipeline that builds the baseline once
187	    (and shares it with discovery) can reuse the identical, hardened Firewall-A loop instead of
188	    rebuilding the baseline. The caller is responsible for having built ``baseline`` blind and
189	    for passing the ``not_after`` ordering instant captured *before* that build — so the
190	    lock-before-baseline guarantee is preserved across the shared-baseline composition.
191	
192	    ``claims`` is **materialized once** here (not re-pulled from a source): a caller that gated
193	    or recorded a claim snapshot must adjudicate *that* snapshot, not a set a re-iterable source
194	    could change between the gate and the scoring (adversarial review #95). Enforces the same
195	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity + pre-registration checks as :func:`adjudicate`.
196	    """
197	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
198	    materialized = list(claims)  # claims used only after the blind baseline is built
199	    # within-run identity uniqueness: a repeated claim_id would be counted once per occurrence in
200	    # _score, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing alignment_rate (#138).
201	    seen_claim_ids: set[str] = set()
202	    for c in materialized:
203	        if c.claim_id in seen_claim_ids:
204	            raise FirewallViolation(
205	                f"duplicate claim_id {c.claim_id!r} within one adjudication run — claim identity "
206	                "must be unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator)"
207	            )
208	        seen_claim_ids.add(c.claim_id)
209	    for claim in materialized:
210	        hypothesis = hypothesis_for(claim)
211	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM:
212	            raise FirewallViolation(
213	                f"claim {claim.claim_id!r} produced a {hypothesis.origin.value!r} hypothesis; "
214	                "adjudicated claims must be EXTERNAL_CLAIM (pre-stated, not data-surfaced)"
215	            )
216	        if hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id:
217	            raise FirewallViolation(
218	                f"hypothesis for claim {claim.claim_id!r} carries source_claim_id "
219	                f"{hypothesis.source_claim_id!r} — claim↔hypothesis misattribution"
220	            )
221	        # Pre-registration: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content (no post-lock content
222	        # swap) and its attested lock time must precede the baseline build (so the claim-derived
223	        # hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline).
224	        require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=not_after)
225	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim)
226	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
227	            raise FirewallViolation(
228	                f"verdict for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} reports hypothesis_id "
229	                f"{verdict.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
230	            )
231	        verdicts.append(verdict)
232	        if on_step is not None:
233	            # per-claim hook (in order) so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND
234	            # its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93).
235	            on_step(hypothesis, verdict)
236	
237	    return _score(source_name, verdicts)
238

1	"""Append-only provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3, W3C PROV-DM style).
2	
3	Every action a study takes is recorded, in order, before the next executes: ingestion, the
4	blind baseline build, each discovered/locked hypothesis, every gate decision, each verdict,
5	the source scorecard, the final report. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of the
6	independence claim — "no step can be retroactively removed or reordered" (GOVERNANCE §3).
7	
8	The trail enforces that structurally, not by promise:
9	
10	- **Append-only.** Entries are added through :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.record` only; there is no
11	  remove/edit/reorder API and the exposed view is an immutable tuple of frozen entries.
12	- **Chained.** Each entry carries ``prev_hash`` and an ``entry_hash`` over
13	  ``(prev_hash, seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp)`` using the engine's one type-faithful
14	  serializer (:mod:`assay_engine._canonical`), rooted at a fixed genesis.
15	
16	Integrity — honest scope, two tiers:
17	
18	- **Unkeyed (default).** The chain is plain SHA-256. It is *tamper-evident against naive
19	  tampering and accidental corruption*: editing one entry without recomputing downstream
20	  hashes, reordering, or deleting an entry is caught by :func:`verify_records`. It is **not**
21	  forgery-proof: a motivated party who controls the serialized bytes can edit a payload and
22	  recompute the whole genesis-rooted chain, and the result verifies. Unkeyed integrity buys
23	  "is this a valid chain?", not "is this *the* chain the engine produced."
24	- **Keyed (``secret=...``).** Pass an on-box secret (as pre-registration does, ADR-0009) and
25	  the chain is HMAC-SHA256. The head cannot be recomputed without the secret, so a downstream
26	  store cannot silently rewrite history and re-seal it; :func:`verify_records` must be given
27	  the same secret. This is local tamper-*resistance*; non-repudiable third-party attestation of
28	  the trail's time remains the same pluggable concern as pre-registration, out of scope here.
29	"""
30	
31	from __future__ import annotations
32	
33	import datetime as _dt
34	import hashlib
35	import hmac
36	import threading
37	from dataclasses import dataclass
38	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
39	
40	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_json
41	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
42	
43	_UTC = _dt.timezone.utc
44	_GENESIS = "assay-provenance:v1:genesis"
45	
46	Clock = Callable[[], _dt.datetime]
47	
48	
49	class ProvenanceError(RuntimeError):
50	    """The provenance trail is inconsistent (a broken chain) or an entry cannot be recorded."""
51	
52	
53	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
54	class ProvenanceEntry:
55	    """One immutable, chained record of a single action."""
56	
57	    seq: int
58	    kind: str
59	    summary: str
60	    payload: Mapping[str, Any]
61	    timestamp: str  # ISO-8601 UTC
62	    prev_hash: str
63	    entry_hash: str
64	
65	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
66	        object.__setattr__(self, "payload", freeze_mapping(self.payload))
67	
68	
69	def _digest(
70	    seq: int,
71	    kind: str,
72	    summary: str,
73	    payload: Mapping[str, Any],
74	    timestamp: str,
75	    prev_hash: str,
76	    secret: bytes | None,
77	) -> str:
78	    try:
79	        msg = canonical_json(
80	            ["assay-prov:v1", prev_hash, seq, kind, summary, dict(payload), timestamp]
81	        ).encode("utf-8")
82	    except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
83	        # RecursionError (deeply nested payload) is a RuntimeError, not Value/TypeError — catch it
84	        # too so the entrypoints surface a typed ProvenanceError, never a raw exception (#89).
85	        raise ProvenanceError(
86	            f"provenance payload for {kind!r} cannot be serialized reproducibly ({exc}); "
87	            "record only JSON-native / hashable values of bounded depth"
88	        ) from None
89	    if secret is None:
90	        return hashlib.sha256(msg).hexdigest()
91	    return hmac.new(secret, msg, "sha256").hexdigest()
92	
93	
94	def _utc_now() -> _dt.datetime:
95	    return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)
96	
97	
98	class ProvenanceTrail:
99	    """An append-only, chained audit trail for one study run.
100	
101	    ``secret`` (>= 16 bytes) keys the chain with HMAC so a serialized trail cannot be forged
102	    without it (see module docstring). ``clock`` injects the timestamp source (deterministic
103	    tests); it must return a timezone-aware ``datetime``.
104	    """
105	
106	    def __init__(self, *, secret: bytes | None = None, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
107	        if secret is not None and (not isinstance(secret, (bytes, bytearray)) or len(secret) < 16):
108	            raise ValueError("provenance secret must be >= 16 bytes of key material")
109	        self._entries: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
110	        self._secret: bytes | None = bytes(secret) if secret is not None else None
111	        self._clock: Clock = clock or _utc_now
112	        # serialize the read-compute-append so concurrent record() calls cannot interleave and
113	        # corrupt the seq/prev_hash chain (#114)
114	        self._lock = threading.Lock()
115	
116	    def record(self, kind: str, summary: str, **payload: Any) -> ProvenanceEntry:
117	        """Append one entry. Returns it. The only way to add to the trail. Thread-safe."""
118	        if not kind:
119	            raise ProvenanceError("provenance entry kind must be non-empty")
120	        with self._lock:
121	            return self._record_locked(kind, summary, payload)
122	
123	    def _record_locked(
124	        self, kind: str, summary: str, payload: Mapping[str, Any]
125	    ) -> ProvenanceEntry:
126	        seq = len(self._entries)
127	        prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
128	        try:
129	            instant = self._clock()
130	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the clock is an injected dependency
131	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance clock raised: {exc}") from None
132	        if not isinstance(instant, _dt.datetime):
133	            raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a datetime")
134	        try:
135	            # utcoffset()/astimezone()/isoformat() can raise on a hostile datetime subclass or an
136	            # out-of-range value — keep the typed-error contract (#96).
137	            if instant.tzinfo is None or instant.utcoffset() is None:
138	                raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a timezone-aware datetime")
139	            timestamp = instant.astimezone(_UTC).isoformat()
140	        except ProvenanceError:
141	            raise
142	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
143	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance timestamp could not be normalized: {exc}") from None
144	        entry_hash = _digest(seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp, prev_hash, self._secret)
145	        try:
146	            entry = ProvenanceEntry(
147	                seq=seq,
148	                kind=kind,
149	                summary=summary,
150	                payload=payload,
151	                timestamp=timestamp,
152	                prev_hash=prev_hash,
153	                entry_hash=entry_hash,
154	            )
155	        except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
156	            raise ProvenanceError(
157	                f"provenance entry for {kind!r} could not be frozen ({exc})"
158	            ) from None
159	        self._entries.append(entry)
160	        return entry
161	
162	    def as_recorder(self) -> Callable[[Any], None]:
163	        """A :data:`gates.ProvenanceRecorder` bound to this trail (records GateDecisions).
164	
165	        Reads each attribute exactly once and defensively, so a booby-trapped decision object
166	        (a property that raises) surfaces a typed ``ProvenanceError`` rather than a raw error.
167	        """
168	
169	        def _record(decision: Any) -> None:
170	            try:
171	                gate = getattr(decision, "gate", None)
172	                approved = bool(getattr(decision, "approved", False))
173	                reason = getattr(decision, "reason", "")
174	                evidence = dict(getattr(decision, "evidence", {}) or {})
175	            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the decision is on the trust boundary
176	                raise ProvenanceError(
177	                    f"could not read gate decision for provenance: {exc}"
178	                ) from None
179	            self.record(
180	                "gate",
181	                f"gate {gate!r}: {'approved' if approved else 'blocked'}",
182	                gate=gate,
183	                approved=approved,
184	                reason=reason,
185	                evidence=evidence,
186	            )
187	
188	        return _record
189	
190	    @property
191	    def entries(self) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
192	        return tuple(self._entries)
193	
194	    @property
195	    def head(self) -> str:
196	        """The current chain head (last entry_hash, or the genesis if empty)."""
197	        return self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
198	
199	    def __len__(self) -> int:
200	        return len(self._entries)
201	
202	    def verify(self) -> None:
203	        """Recompute the chain; raise :class:`ProvenanceError` on any inconsistency."""
204	        verify_records(self._entries, secret=self._secret)
205	
206	    def to_records(self) -> tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]:
207	        """Serialize to plain dicts (for a persistent store); re-checkable via :func:`verify_records`."""
208	        return tuple(
209	            {
210	                "seq": e.seq,
211	                "kind": e.kind,
212	                "summary": e.summary,
213	                "payload": dict(e.payload),
214	                "timestamp": e.timestamp,
215	                "prev_hash": e.prev_hash,
216	                "entry_hash": e.entry_hash,
217	            }
218	            for e in self._entries
219	        )
220	
221	
222	def verify_records(
223	    entries: "list[ProvenanceEntry] | tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]",
224	    *,
225	    secret: bytes | None = None,
226	) -> None:
227	    """Verify a sequence of entries forms an intact chain (order, linkage, content).
228	
229	    Pass the same ``secret`` the trail was keyed with; an unkeyed trail verifies with
230	    ``secret=None``. A keyed trail will not verify under the wrong/absent secret.
231	    """
232	    prev = _GENESIS
233	    for i, e in enumerate(entries):
234	        if e.seq != i:
235	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} has seq {e.seq} (reordered or removed)")
236	        if e.prev_hash != prev:
237	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} prev_hash breaks the chain")
238	        recomputed = _digest(e.seq, e.kind, e.summary, e.payload, e.timestamp, e.prev_hash, secret)
239	        if not hmac.compare_digest(recomputed, e.entry_hash):
240	            raise ProvenanceError(
241	                f"provenance entry {i} content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)"
242	            )
243	        prev = e.entry_hash
244	
245	
246	def from_records(
247	    records: "list[Mapping[str, Any]] | tuple[Mapping[str, Any], ...]",
248	    *,
249	    secret: bytes | None = None,
250	) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
251	    """Rebuild entries from :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` output and verify the chain."""
252	    entries = tuple(
253	        ProvenanceEntry(
254	            seq=r["seq"],
255	            kind=r["kind"],
256	            summary=r["summary"],
257	            payload=r["payload"],
258	            timestamp=r["timestamp"],
259	            prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
260	            entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
261	        )
262	        for r in records
263	    )
264	    verify_records(entries, secret=secret)
265	    return entries
266

1	"""Dependency-light, deterministic numeric primitives — the baseline's raw material.
2	
3	These are the small, exact, NumPy-free building blocks the methodology calls "raw material"
4	for a baseline: similarity/distance and descriptive statistics over a
5	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.features.FeatureMatrix`. They are deterministic (no RNG, no
6	iteration-order dependence) and pure, so a study can assemble a simple baseline — and the
7	engine can unit-test these invariants — with no heavy dependency.
8	
9	Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring
10	their own builders implementing :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`; the
11	engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002). Heavier engine helpers may be
12	added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays dependency-free.
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import math
18	from typing import Any, Sequence
19	
20	Vector = Sequence[float]
21	
22	
23	def _check_same_length(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> None:
24	    if len(a) != len(b):
25	        raise ValueError(f"vectors must have equal length; got {len(a)} and {len(b)}")
26	
27	
28	def _check_finite(*vectors: Vector) -> None:
29	    """Reject non-finite components. Without this a NaN/inf could flow into cosine and be
30	    silently clamped to a false 1.0 (audit #B6); consistent with descriptive_stats."""
31	    for vec in vectors:
32	        for x in vec:
33	            if not math.isfinite(x):
34	                raise ValueError(f"vector components must be finite; got {x}")
35	
36	
37	def dot(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
38	    _check_same_length(a, b)
39	    return math.fsum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
40	
41	
42	def l2_norm(a: Vector) -> float:
43	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum(x * x for x in a))
44	
45	
46	def _clamp_unit(x: float) -> float:
47	    """Clamp to [-1, 1] to absorb floating-point overshoot (e.g. self-similarity 1.0000…002)."""
48	    return max(-1.0, min(1.0, x))
49	
50	
51	def cosine_similarity(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
52	    """Cosine similarity in [-1, 1]. A zero vector has no direction, so its similarity to
53	    anything is defined as 0.0 (rather than NaN). The result is clamped to [-1, 1] so float
54	    rounding cannot push it outside the documented range (audit #B3)."""
55	    _check_same_length(a, b)
56	    _check_finite(a, b)
57	    na, nb = l2_norm(a), l2_norm(b)
58	    if na == 0.0 or nb == 0.0:
59	        return 0.0
60	    return _clamp_unit(dot(a, b) / (na * nb))
61	
62	
63	def euclidean_distance(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
64	    _check_same_length(a, b)
65	    _check_finite(a, b)
66	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum((x - y) ** 2 for x, y in zip(a, b)))
67	
68	
69	def _cosine_matrix_pure(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
70	    """Pure-Python reference implementation — O(n²·d). Correct but slow; the public function
71	    dispatches to a numpy path at scale when available."""
72	    n = len(rows)
73	    _check_finite(*rows)
74	    norms = [l2_norm(r) for r in rows]
75	    out = [[0.0] * n for _ in range(n)]
76	    for i in range(n):
77	        for j in range(i, n):
78	            if norms[i] == 0.0 or norms[j] == 0.0:
79	                sim = 0.0
80	            elif i == j:
81	                sim = 1.0  # a non-zero vector's self-similarity is exactly 1.0 (no float drift)
82	            else:
83	                sim = _clamp_unit(dot(rows[i], rows[j]) / (norms[i] * norms[j]))
84	            out[i][j] = sim
85	            out[j][i] = sim
86	    return out
87	
88	
89	def _cosine_ndarray(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
90	    """Compute the cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray (no nested-list boxing)."""
91	    import numpy as np
92	
93	    x = np.asarray(rows, dtype=float)
94	    if x.ndim != 2:
95	        raise ValueError("rows must be a 2-D collection of equal-length vectors")
96	    if not np.isfinite(x).all():
97	        raise ValueError("vector components must be finite")
98	    norms = np.linalg.norm(x, axis=1)
99	    if not np.isfinite(norms).all():
100	        # finite inputs can still overflow when squared (||x||) on huge-magnitude vectors;
101	        # reject rather than silently produce inf/inf similarities (audit N1; restores parity
102	        # with the pure path, which raises on the same input).
103	        raise ValueError("vector norm overflow — component magnitudes are too large")
104	    zero = norms == 0.0
105	    safe = np.where(zero, 1.0, norms)
106	    xn = x / safe[:, None]
107	    m = xn @ xn.T
108	    np.clip(m, -1.0, 1.0, out=m)
109	    if zero.any():  # zero vectors have no direction → similarity 0 to everything
110	        m[zero, :] = 0.0
111	        m[:, zero] = 0.0
112	    np.fill_diagonal(m, np.where(zero, 0.0, 1.0))
113	    return m
114	
115	
116	def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
117	    return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in _cosine_ndarray(rows)]
118	
119	
120	def cosine_similarity_matrix_array(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
121	    """The cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray — no nested-list boxing (#106).
122	
123	    For large corpora the nested-list form of :func:`cosine_similarity_matrix` multiplies peak
124	    memory several-fold; a builder operating at scale should keep the ndarray and store it in the
125	    baseline's ``contents`` (the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested
126	    list). Requires numpy (the ``baseline`` extra).
127	    """
128	    try:
129	        import numpy as np
130	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
131	        raise RuntimeError(
132	            "cosine_similarity_matrix_array requires numpy (the 'baseline' extra)"
133	        ) from exc
134	    if not rows:
135	        return np.empty((0, 0), dtype=float)
136	    return _cosine_ndarray(rows)
137	
138	
139	def cosine_similarity_matrix(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
140	    """Full symmetric cosine-similarity matrix (diagonal 1.0 for non-zero vectors, 0.0 for zero).
141	
142	    Pure-Python is O(n²·d) and does not scale (a corpus of thousands of embeddings would take
143	    minutes-to-hours); when numpy is available (the optional ``baseline`` extra) this dispatches
144	    to a vectorized path that is orders of magnitude faster. The two paths agree to within
145	    floating-point tolerance (both clamp to [-1, 1]); a study needing byte-identical
146	    reproducibility should pin its environment (the determinism harness records component
147	    versions for exactly this reason).
148	    """
149	    if not rows:
150	        return []
151	    try:
152	        import numpy  # noqa: F401
153	    except ImportError:
154	        return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)
155	    return _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows)
156	
157	
158	def _quantile(sorted_values: list[float], q: float) -> float:
159	    """Linear-interpolation quantile (``q`` in [0, 1]) over already-sorted values."""
160	    if not 0.0 <= q <= 1.0:
161	        raise ValueError(f"quantile q must be in [0, 1]; got {q}")
162	    n = len(sorted_values)
163	    if n == 1:
164	        return sorted_values[0]
165	    pos = q * (n - 1)
166	    lo = math.floor(pos)
167	    hi = math.ceil(pos)
168	    if lo == hi:
169	        return sorted_values[lo]
170	    frac = pos - lo
171	    return sorted_values[lo] * (1 - frac) + sorted_values[hi] * frac
172	
173	
174	def descriptive_stats(values: Sequence[float]) -> dict[str, float]:
175	    """Descriptive statistics of ``values``.
176	
177	    Returns ``n`` (count), ``mean``, ``std`` (sample std, ddof=1; ``0.0`` for a single value
178	    rather than NaN), ``min``, ``max``, and ``q25``/``median``/``q75`` (linear-interpolation
179	    quantiles). Raises on an empty input or any non-finite value.
180	    """
181	    if not values:
182	        raise ValueError("descriptive_stats requires at least one value")
183	    n = len(values)
184	    for v in values:
185	        if not math.isfinite(v):
186	            raise ValueError(f"values must be finite; got {v}")
187	    mean = math.fsum(values) / n
188	    if n == 1:
189	        std = 0.0
190	    else:
191	        var = math.fsum((v - mean) ** 2 for v in values) / (n - 1)
192	        std = math.sqrt(var)
193	    s = sorted(values)
194	    return {
195	        "n": float(n),
196	        "mean": mean,
197	        "std": std,
198	        "min": s[0],
199	        "max": s[-1],
200	        "q25": _quantile(s, 0.25),
201	        "median": _quantile(s, 0.5),
202	        "q75": _quantile(s, 0.75),
203	    }

1	"""Study runner — the composed, governed, end-to-end pipeline (ADR-0010).
2	
3	The phase machine (:mod:`assay_engine.orchestration.phases`) *names* the ordered stages a
4	study moves through; the firewall runners enforce the methodology; the seams provide tracing,
5	experiment tracking, data versioning, and (in the adapter's callables) reasoning. This module
6	composes them into a single runnable flow, engine-owned so the ordering and the governance
7	handoffs are guaranteed once, not re-implemented per study:
8	
9	    INGEST → BASELINE(blind, Firewall A) → [DISCOVERY → PREREGISTER → gate → CONFIRM (Firewall B)]
10	           → [ADJUDICATE → SCORE (Firewall A, shared baseline)] → REPORT
11	
12	At every step it (a) records an append-only provenance entry *before the next step runs*
13	(GOVERNANCE §3), (b) opens a trace span (observability seam), optionally (c) versions the source
14	and logs to an experiment tracker, and (d) enforces the methodological invariants structurally:
15	the baseline is built blind, the split ids are validated against the corpus, hypotheses are
16	verifiably pre-registered before confirmation, a human gate reviews before *every* confirmatory
17	step, claim-derived hypotheses are locked before the baseline exists, and the visited phase
18	sequence must equal the mode's required sequence.
19	
20	A study supplies the *domain* via a :class:`StudyPlan` (parser, baseline builder, the
21	discover/confirm/hypothesis_for callables, the claims source, optional feature builders); the
22	engine owns the *order, the firewalls, the gate, the provenance, the tracing, and the
23	versioning*. Reasoning enters only inside the study's own callables (which may use
24	:mod:`assay_engine.reasoning`); any such LLM output is *judgment*, so it should be wrapped as an
25	:class:`~assay_engine.methodology.fence.Interpretation` and never fed back into a measurement
26	(the fence). The runner imports no adapter (ARCHITECTURE §3).
27	"""

109	def _engine_claim_fingerprint(claim_records: list[ClaimRecord]) -> str:
110	    """Engine-computed content hash of the EXACT claim set that will be scored (#137).
111	
112	    The source's self-reported ``claim_fingerprint()`` is an unverified self-report — it can say
113	    anything. The engine therefore computes its own type-faithful hash over the materialized
114	    records (in scored order) and records THAT as the authoritative fingerprint, so an auditor
115	    re-deriving the fingerprint from the recorded/scored claims gets the value the trail records.
116	    """
117	    return hash_value(
118	        [
119	            {
120	                "claim_id": c.claim_id,
121	                "subject": c.subject,
122	                "referents": list(c.referents),
123	                "assertion": c.assertion,
124	                "provenance": c.provenance,
125	            }
126	            for c in claim_records
127	        ]
128	    )

131	def _require_unique_ids(ids: list[str], what: str) -> None:
132	    """Raise FirewallViolation if ``ids`` contains a duplicate (#138).
133	
134	    Identity (claim↔hypothesis↔verdict) must be unique within a run: a repeated id would
135	    otherwise be counted once per occurrence, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing
136	    alignment_rate / verdict accounting.
137	    """
138	    seen: set[str] = set()
139	    dupes: list[str] = []
140	    for i in ids:
141	        if i in seen:
142	            dupes.append(i)
143	        seen.add(i)
144	    if dupes:
145	        raise FirewallViolation(
146	            f"duplicate {what} within one run: {sorted(set(dupes))[:3]} — identity must be "
147	            "unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator / verdict accounting)"
148	        )

151	def auto_approve(review: GateReview) -> GateDecision:
152	    """A non-interactive gate handler: approve and record the decision.
153	
154	    This is the explicit "no operator" choice — it is **not** the default, so a study cannot
155	    silently bypass human review by omitting a handler (it must consciously pass this). Replace
156	    with an operator-driven handler (e.g. one wired to the LangGraph gate interrupt/resume in
157	    :mod:`assay_engine.orchestration`) for genuine human-in-the-loop parking; the handler may
158	    return ``approved=False`` to block the transition.
159	    """
160	    return GateDecision(
161	        approved=True,
162	        gate=review.gate,
163	        reason="non-interactive auto-approval",
164	        evidence={"transition": f"{review.frm.name}->{review.to.name}"},
165	    )
